sentences.base_language: Good morning! Are you ready for the meeting?
sentences.target_language: صباح الخير! هل أنت مستعد للاجتماع؟
sentences.romanized: sabah al-khayr! hal anta musta'idd lil-ijtima'?
sentences.grammarNote: الاِجتِمَاع (al-ijtimāʿ, the meeting) — Form VIII verbal noun of ج-م-ع (j-m-ʿ, to gather): اِجتَمَعَ = to meet/gather (together). لِلاِجتِمَاع (lil-ijtimāʿ, for the meeting) — لِـ (for) + الاجتماع. هَل أَنتَ مُستَعِدّ (hal anta mustaʿidd, are you ready) — a nominal yes/no question: هَل + nominal sentence. مُستَعِدّ is the Form X active participle of ع-د-د.

sentences.base_language: Yes, I prepared the report yesterday.
sentences.grammarNote: حَضَّرتُ (ḥaḍḍartu, I prepared) — Form II of ح-ض-ر (ḥ-ḍ-r, to be present): حَضَّرَ = to prepare/make ready (causative). Compare Form I حَضَرَ (to arrive/be present). التَّقرِير (at-taqrīr, the report) — Form II verbal noun of ق-ر-ر (q-r-r, to decide/state): قَرَّرَ (to decide), تَقرِير (report — a stating of facts). أَمس (ams, yesterday) — an indeclinable time adverb.

sentences.base_language: The meeting starts at nine o'clock.
sentences.grammarNote: يَبدَأ (yabdaʾ, it begins) — Form I of ب-د-أ (b-d-ʾ, to begin): بَدَأَ. فِي التَّاسِعَة (fī at-tāsiʿa, at the ninth hour = at nine) — Arabic tells time with ordinal numbers: التَّاسِعَة (the ninth, feminine) = nine o'clock. Ordinals for telling time are always feminine because they agree with الساعة (the hour, feminine), even when الساعة is omitted.

sentences.base_language: The manager wants to discuss the new project.
sentences.grammarNote: المُدِير (al-mudīr, the manager) — active participle Form IV of د-و-ر (d-w-r, to turn/manage): أَدَارَ = to manage, مُدِير = one who manages. مُنَاقَشَة (munāqasha, discussion) — Form III verbal noun of ن-ق-ش (n-q-sh, to discuss/engrave): نَاقَشَ = to discuss with someone. المَشرُوع الجَدِيد (al-mashrūʿ al-jadīd, the new project) — definite noun + definite adjective.

sentences.base_language: How many people are on your team?
sentences.grammarNote: كَم شَخصاً (kam shakṣan, how many people) — كَم (how many) + indefinite accusative noun with tanwin: شَخصاً (a person, accusative). This is the standard interrogative structure for counting: كَم + noun in the accusative with tanwin. فَرِيقَك (farīqak, your team) — فَرِيق (team/group, from root ف-ر-ق, to separate/divide) + suffix كَ (your m.).

sentences.base_language: We have five engineers and two designers.
sentences.grammarNote: لَدَينَا (ladaynā, we have) — لَدى + suffix نَا (we/our). خَمسَة مُهَندِسِين (khamsa muhandisīn, five engineers) — خَمسَة takes the feminine ة because the noun مُهَندِسِين is masculine (gender polarity rule for 3–10). مُهَندِسِين is the sound masculine plural genitive/accusative of مُهَندِس (engineer). مُصَمِّمَان (muṣammimān, two designers) — dual nominative of مُصَمِّم (designer, Form II active participle of ص-م-م, to design).

sentences.base_language: I send many emails every day.
sentences.target_language: أرسل رسائل بريد إلكتروني كثيرة كل يوم.
sentences.romanized: ursil rasa'il barid iliktruní kathira kull yawm.
sentences.literal: I-send messages mail electronic many every day.
sentences.grammarNote: أُرسِل (ursil, I send) — Form IV of ر-س-ل (r-s-l, to send): أَرسَلَ = to send. رَسَائِل (rasāʾil, messages) — broken plural of رِسَالَة (letter/message), pattern فَعَائِل. بَرِيد إِلِكتُرُونِيّ (barīd iliktrūnī, electronic mail/email) — an iḍāfa with an adjective: بَرِيد (mail) from root ب-ر-د (to be cold — postal horses rested at برد = relay stations). كَثِيرَة (kathīra, many — feminine) agrees with رَسَائِل (which takes feminine agreement as a broken plural of inanimates).

sentences.base_language: My colleague helps me with difficult tasks.
sentences.grammarNote: زَمِيلِي (zamīlī, my colleague) — زَمِيل (colleague, فَعِيل pattern from root ز-م-ل, to accompany) + suffix ي (my). يُسَاعِدُنِي (yusāʿidunī, he helps me) — Form III of س-ع-د (s-ʿ-d): Form III implies interaction. المَهَامّ الصَّعبَة (al-mahāmm aṣ-ṣaʿba, the difficult tasks) — المَهَامّ is the broken plural of مُهِمَّة (task/mission, Form IV active participle of ه-م-م). الصَّعبَة (difficult, feminine) agrees with the plural المهام.

sentences.base_language: I finish work at five in the afternoon.
sentences.grammarNote: أُنهِي (unhī, I finish) — Form IV of ن-ه-ي (n-h-y, to end/forbid): أَنهَى = to finish/conclude. الخَامِسَة (al-khāmisa, the fifth hour = five o'clock) — feminine ordinal, agreeing with الساعة (hour). مَسَاءً (masāʾan, in the afternoon/evening) — indefinite accusative time adverb with tanwin. العَمَل (al-ʿamal, work) from root ع-م-ل: عَمَلَ (to work), عَامِل (worker), عَمَل (work).

sentences.base_language: After work, I like to relax at home.
sentences.target_language: بعد العمل، أحب الاسترخاء في البيت.
sentences.romanized: ba'd al-amal, uhibb al-istirkha' fi al-bayt.
sentences.grammarNote: بعد (ba'd) = after. In Arabic, بعد + definite noun is common: بعد العمل = after work, بعد الاجتماع = after the meeting.

sentences.base_language: My job is challenging but rewarding.
sentences.grammarNote: وَظِيفَتِي (waẓīfatī, my job) — وَظِيفَة (job/function, from root و-ظ-ف, to assign a role) + possessive suffix ي (my). The ة becomes ت before the suffix. لكنَّها مُجزِيَة (lakinnahā mujziya, but it is rewarding) — لكنَّ (but, emphatic) + هَا (it, feminine) referring to وَظِيفَة. مُجزِيَة (mujziya, rewarding, feminine) — Form IV active participle of ج-ز-ي (j-z-y, to reward/compensate): أَجزَى = to reward.

مَا أَفعَلُه (mā afʿaluhu, what I do) — مَا (what, relative pronoun introducing a clause) + أَفعَل (I do) + suffix هُ (it). Arabic uses مَا as a relative pronoun for non-human or general referents: مَا تُحِبّ = what you love. The suffix هُ on the verb is a resumptive pronoun referring back to مَا — a standard feature of Arabic relative clauses.
